text: I am writing because my plan — Silver Home Broadband 24 — has increased in price mid-contract and I am finding the whole situation very worrying. I do not understand the technicalities and have phoned four times over the past three weeks (5 March, 12 March, 19 March and 23 March); each time I was told someone would get back to me but no one has. On 19 March I spoke with Claire who suggested that the increase was ‘standard,’ which is not an adequate explanation for changing contractual terms without clear notice. I am elderly, not confident online, and rely on clear, patient communication. Please either honour the original contract rate or allow me to leave without penalty; I would appreciate a written confirmation and simple step-by-step guidance on how to proceed if cancellation is necessary.

I am writing after repeated unsuccessful attempts to resolve what appears to be fraudulent activity on my family account. Timeline: on 28 March I received a text indicating a SIM change for one of my children’s numbers; I did not authorise this. On 29 March I noticed two outgoing high-value calls billed to an unfamiliar premium-rate service totalling £960. I reported the unauthorised SIM swap to your support line on 30 March (call ref 90512) and was told a fraud investigation would start — I was given an incident number, F-00377, and advised to contact my bank. I followed up by email on 2 April and via web chat on 5 April (chat ref 332188) because no holds had been placed and new charges continued to appear. On 7 April I spoke briefly to a specialist named Mark in fraud who said he would escalate to the fraud team manager; I have not had a substantive update since then. I have filed a police report (station: Hounslow, ref 2024-14839) and my bank has provisionally blocked the affected card, but the continuing exposure on the account presents a real risk to my family’s finances and personal data. Please take the following immediate actions: freeze any further account changes, restore any altered security settings, reverse the fraudulent charges pending your investigation, and supply a written timeline of the steps you will take and the expected completion date. If you are unable to confirm these actions within 48 hours I will need to escalate this to the ombudsman and pursue formal complaints through the police report and my bank. I am calm about the process but require urgent and decisive action to prevent further harm.

On 02 March 2026 I telephoned your sales line to sign up for a student plan and spoke with an agent named Jack. He described a 12-month plan with 40GB of data at £15 per month and reassured me twice that there would be no early termination fees and that content filters would be applied for parental control. Based on that conversation I accepted and gave my bank details. The confirmation email I received that evening, however, shows a 24-month contract at £35 per month and no filters; it also includes an early termination charge. I called sales back on 04 March and spoke to Hannah who admitted there had been a mistake and said she would escalate to complaints. On 10 March I called again, spoke to Paul in complaints, who promised a manager would call me within five working days; no manager has called and I have received only automated updates. I am a student with very limited funds and the difference between what was promised and what has been applied is unaffordable. I have exhausted the normal channels and am now requesting a manager review and correction of the contract to the terms promised by Jack on 02 March. If that is not resolved promptly I will ask for intervention by the Ombudsman. Please provide the name of the manager handling this and a firm timescale for correction.

I am a small business owner and my case has been mishandled for too long. On 2 March I reported a billing error where a duplicate charge of £310 appeared on the account; I was promised a refund by 12 March by adviser Harpreet. That date passed and I called again on 15 March, was transferred three times, given two different reference numbers and told someone would call back — no call came. I escalated on 20 March and spoke to Emma who said the refund was authorised, yet my bank statement shows nothing. This complaint has been passed around with contradictory information and no resolution. I have exhausted the usual routes and now request a named manager to review the file, confirm the refund in writing, and provide a timeline for when I will see the money. If I do not receive a satisfactory reply within five working days I will submit a formal complaint to the ombudsman and include all correspondence.

I cancelled my contract and switched provider on 05/02/2026. Your records are continuing to debit my bank account. A direct debit was taken on 10/03/2026 despite clear notice of cancellation and confirmation emails from the new supplier. I have contacted your billing teams repeatedly: I spoke with Claire on 12/03/2026 who advised a refund; I spoke with Michael on 18/03/2026 who said the matter was resolved; neither outcome occurred.

This is a formal notice: the charges taken after termination are unlawful. Under the Consumer Rights framework and your own terms, continuation of billing post-cancellation constitutes an unlawful deprivation of funds. I demand immediate repayment of all sums debited since 06/02/2026, a written explanation of why the direct debit mandate was not cancelled, and evidence that my account has been closed. Provide the refund within seven days and supply a named manager to handle this matter.

If you do not comply I will escalate to the Ombudsman and consider a claim in the Small Claims Court and referral to Trading Standards. I have exhausted standard channels and am requesting escalation to a manager and to the Ombudsman now. Please treat this as time-critical and respond in writing within 48 hours.

When I switched from ClearWave on 02/02 I expected a smooth transfer. Instead I spent the next six weeks unwinding a mess that began with a sales call on 02/02 from an advisor who gave his name as Liam. He assured me I was being put onto the ‘All-Inclusive Family’ tariff with unlimited calls and 500GB data. I signed up based on that assurance. On 12/03 I received a bill showing pay-as-you-go charges totalling £38 for calls and texts I believed to be included. I called support three times (cases 112233, 112244 and 112255) and spoke to Priya, then Marcus, then a chat agent who said a manager would call but nobody ever did. I also visited your King’s Cross shop on 18/03 and left with no resolution. This has cost me both time and money; what began as wanting to consolidate accounts now feels like being boxed into the wrong product because of a mis-sold plan. I have exhausted the standard channels and request this be escalated to a manager immediately. If we cannot agree a correction and a refund of the erroneous charges I will pursue an independent review through the ombudsman. Please confirm corrective action and an itemised credit to my account within five working days.

This is getting tedious. I was sold the 'Unlimited Home Fibre' plan over the phone on 4 February 2026 by an agent named Ben Carter (call record 04/02/2026, ref 102334). During that call I explicitly told him I wanted unlimited data at £25/month and no fixed-term exit penalties. Ben confirmed this deal and said the order was placed. Instead, my contract shows 'Business Lite' at £45/month with a 24-month minimum term — a plan I never accepted and which is unsuitable for my household. I raised this with support on 10/02 (chat ref 11001), 18/02 (phone ref 11444) and 02/03 (email to billing, ref 11987). Each time I have been given different explanations: billing system error, policy reasons, or promises that the paperwork would be amended. No correction has been made and I have been charged the higher monthly fee for March (£45.00 taken on 01/03/2026).

I am a busy professional and have already spent hours on calls and emails. To be specific, I require: a) immediate reversion to the product I was sold (Unlimited Home Fibre) at the £25/month rate; b) a full refund/credit for the excess amounts charged since activation; c) removal of any early-termination obligation arising from the incorrect contract; and d) written confirmation of these corrections and the timescale for completion. Please escalate to a senior billing manager and resolve within five working days. If you cannot fix this promptly I will request a formal investigation and pursue compensation for my time and the incorrect billing.

I am writing as the primary account holder for a family bundle with four lines (account reference 79-3045). Over the past three weeks I have identified a sequence of fees which were not disclosed at the point of renewal: a so-called 'admin charge' of £17.95 applied on 03 March, and a recurring 'service management' fee of £4.99 applied from 10 March. I have called customer services on 4 separate occasions (14 March, 17 March, 21 March and 23 March) and spoken with agents Rina Patel and a supervisor in billing, who logged references BIL-1423 and BIL-2147. Each time I was told that the charges were legitimate and that I would receive an itemised breakdown by email; that breakdown has not arrived.

As a parent managing multiple lines, transparent billing is essential. I require a full explanation in writing of the nature and contractual basis for these fees, a full refund of all unagreed charges applied since 1 March 2026, and confirmation that no such fees will be levied in future without express consent. Please respond within seven working days with the documentation I requested. If you cannot substantiate these charges, I expect them removed immediately and a written apology sent to my email address on file.

My name is Margaret Davies and I am 78. I try to manage my single mobile line on a fixed pension but your March bill is wrong and I don’t know what to do. I received invoice 003287 dated 12 March 2026 showing £97.45 due. My contract is for a £21.99 monthly plan plus £1.49 line rental; I normally pay £23.48. There are four extra items labelled as "premium content" on 02/03, 06/03, 08/03 and 11/03 totalling £69.97. I do not subscribe to any services. My granddaughter, Jenna, checked my phone settings on 13 March and there were apps installed that I did not put there. I cannot understand the billing portal — the fonts are small and the layout confuses me. This is the first time I have complained to you. I do not have savings to cover unexpected charges. Please do the following: (1) remove the premium items, (2) refund £69.97 to my debit card immediately, (3) put a block on my account so no further premium purchases can be made without my explicit spoken permission, and (4) send me a clear, printed statement to my home address at 17 Willow Road, Norwich NR3 4LP explaining what happened in plain language I can understand. If you need to call, please ask for my granddaughter Jenna who helps me — her mobile is 07788 332244. I am frightened about being billed again and I need this fixed straight away.

I need to cancel one line on our family account and would like that process completed promptly. On 22 March I used the online cancellation form but did not receive a confirmation number, and the line remains active with a further payment due on 1 April for £27.50. As the account holder and parent, I require the line to be ended and any future billing for that line stopped; please confirm cancellation and provide a refund for the pro rata amount already charged for April. I expect written confirmation of cancellation and the date it will take effect.

My name is Dorothy Kemp and I am writing because my direct debit was taken for another year of the package I explicitly told your adviser in February I did not want to renew. I do not understand how this happens. I first rang on 2 February to ask about canceling; I spoke to Sophie and she said she would note my account. On 15 February I called again after noticing an email I did not understand; Mark told me everything was fine. On 1 March I saw a new charge of £28.99 on my bank statement and I phoned straight away — I spoke to Lucy who told me the system shows an automatic renewal and referred me to your app. I do not use the app. I am 78 and not confident with smartphones or online forms; I told every person that and yet my card was debited without my permission. I am upset and exhausted by having to explain myself repeatedly over several weeks. I want the renewal reversed and a clear note on my account that I want no further renewals. Please do not ask me to use the app; arrange the correction by phone or post. If you need to call, please call during weekday mornings when my hearing aid is switched on — I find evening automated systems impossible. Thank you.

So you cancelled my line on 2 March 2026, which is helpful, because I expressly asked for the family plan to be pared back. Your website even sent me a cancellation confirmation reference: CF-83912. Imagine my surprise to see the direct debit taken on 25 March for £42.50 for the very line I asked to remove. I appreciate systems can be slow; I also appreciate that charging for a service that was confirmed as cancelled is not best practice. Please reverse the payment and confirm the refund date, and while you are at it update the account so the rest of the family plan is charged correctly. I am a parent juggling school runs and work; handling billing errors offers me no pleasure, only inconvenience. I expect a correction on the next statement and a brief note explaining what went wrong.

I reported a faulty handset on 5 March after my replacement phone started randomly losing network and dropping calls mid-transaction. I spoke to Hannah on your support line who logged the fault and promised a courier replacement within 48 hours. It is now 22 March and nothing has arrived, the phone continues to cut out every day and I am losing business opportunities. I have made one previous attempt to resolve this and was given the runaround; I am not prepared to accept another vague promise. I need either a working handset dispatched by recorded delivery today, or a credit for the days I have been without reliable service. I run a small trade business and rely on a functioning phone to take orders. Please stop delaying and do what was agreed with Hannah on 5 March.

I am attempting to cancel my broadband and mobile bundle because I can no longer afford the monthly payments as a student living on a tight budget. I started the cancellation process via your app on 14/03/2026 and was repeatedly blocked by an error message that simply returns to the homepage. I then tried the online chat on 15/03/2026 where the agent advised me to call the cancellations line; the agent said there would be no early termination charge if I provided evidence of financial hardship, but when I called on 16/03/2026 the automated options routed me into a lengthy queue and I had to leave for a lecture after a 40-minute wait. The app also shows a pending bill of £42.30 for 01/04/2026 which I cannot afford if I am still tied into a monthly charge. I am asking you to cancel my service, confirm whether any termination fee applies, and if a fee is charged to consider a reduction or waiver given my student circumstances. Please respond with the straightforward steps I need to follow to confirm cancellation without additional financial burden and a firm date by which the account will be closed and final charges processed.

I raised the early termination fee issue once already on 12 March with a representative named Ben, and since apparently the terms of my contract are more accommodating to sudden life changes than the customer service notes suggest, here I am again. My doctor has confirmed a change in my mobility (documents submitted on 15 March) and I am moving to a residence that your coverage cannot reach. The agent on 12/03 told me the fee would be reviewed; I was then presented with a charge of £425.00 on my account on 18 March.

I am asking, for the second time, that you waive this fee on compassionate and practical grounds. I would prefer not to escalate, but I cannot reasonably be expected to pay this sum when the service is no longer usable for me. Please confirm a waiver or offer a reasonable settlement within seven days.

Account number 455-781-992: I am disputing recurring overcharges that have been billed since 1 February. The monthly plan on my contract is £39.50 but invoices for February, March and April show £56.42, £56.42 and £76.42 respectively. I have contacted you on 2 February, 9 February, 23 February and most recently 18 March — five separate contacts — yet the charges persist. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and the terms of my contract, I request an immediate correction of the account, a full refund of the overcharged amounts (£49.76 total), a written itemised bill for the disputed period and confirmation of any remedial adjustments to my tariff. I also require an explanation of why automated billing failed to reflect my agreed plan and what measures you will take to prevent recurrence. If I do not receive a substantive written response within 14 days I will consider escalation to the Ombudsman Services and seek interest on the refunded sums.

I am contacting you as the account holder for a family plan (three lines) to report a faulty handset and to request a prompt remedy. The handset in question is listed on the account as my daughter's device, a Huawei P30 with IMEI ending 4827, purchased through your online store on 11 February 2025. Since 9 March 2026 the device has exhibited persistent problems: the screen intermittently fails to register touch input, applications freeze, and the phone sometimes reboots without warning. These faults make it difficult for my daughter to use school apps and to receive messages from the family group, although there is no immediate safety issue.

This is the first time I have raised this matter with you. Please confirm whether this device is covered under the 24-month manufacturer or retailer warranty, and advise whether you will provide a repair, a replacement handset, or a temporary loan device while any repairs are undertaken. I would appreciate written confirmation of the next steps and an estimated timeframe for resolution. You can reach me on the account mobile 07xxx xxxxxx or by email at myaddress@example.com. Thank you for attending to this matter; I would prefer a clear schedule for repair rather than open-ended delays.

Cancelled my line on 14/03/2026 but your bill still charged £27.99 on 28/03. I called once on 30/03 and spoke to Tom who said it would be sorted but I have had nothing back. I am elderly and this is confusing and stressful. Refund the £27.99 and confirm cancellation immediately. A simple clear reply, please.

I have been a customer since 2010 (account 00457219) and am escalating this matter because your frontline teams have failed to resolve a complaint after multiple attempts. The original issue — repeated incorrect billing and failure to apply the loyalty discount promised when I renewed in January 2026 — was raised with Tom Harris on 2026-02-10 and again with Priya on 2026-02-18. I submitted a formal complaint by email on 2026-02-25 and received an acknowledgement; complaints reference CMP-9981 was opened on 2026-03-05 with a 10-day resolution target that has lapsed.

Since then I have had three further callbacks where I was given inconsistent information, promised a manager callback that never arrived, and provided with different compensation figures. I am no longer willing to be passed between teams. As a long-standing customer I expect your complaints procedure to be handled competently and within the published timeframes. I now require escalation to a senior complaints manager and a written response setting out: (a) why the loyalty discount was not applied; (b) a corrected account statement; and (c) the compensation you will pay for the service and handling failures. If I do not receive a satisfactory response within seven days I will refer this matter to the Ombudsman and consider terminating my services and seeking redress for breach of contract.

I joined on 3 February on your 'Fibre 80' tariff and have repeatedly measured downstream speeds of 8–12 Mbps during off-peak and peak times (tests on 9 March and 18 March using speedtest.net). I raised this with your agent (reference 20260309-AL) on 10 March and was told it would be investigated; nothing has been resolved. Under the Consumer Rights Act and Ofcom labeling guidance I expect the service to meet the advertised reasonable performance. If you cannot supply a stable speed commensurate with the tariff within 14 days I will require either a proportionate price reduction, a technical remediation plan with milestones, or a contract termination without penalty. Please confirm the steps you will take and provide the technical evidence you will rely on for assessment. I expect an acknowledgement within 48 hours and a substantive response within 10 working days.

I have been a customer for over fourteen years and I am fed up with my handset continually failing. The device supplied by you under my current line stopped holding a charge and the microphone cuts out during calls. This began around 1 March and I logged faults on 5 March, 12 March, and again on 20 March (tickets 90543, 91210, 91987). Each time I spoke with different agents — Naomi first advised a factory reset, Lee later promised a loan phone and then that promise was not fulfilled. The phone now switches off mid-conversation and yesterday I lost an important work call because the other party couldn't hear me. I need either a replacement device of the same specification or a full refund for the handset, plus compensation for the lost work day. I have tried your basic troubleshooting repeatedly; the problem is hardware-related and has not been fixed despite multiple contacts over the last three weeks. Please resolve this without further delay.

I switched over from my previous provider on 1 April and the package was meant to be active from that day. The router shows no sync and I was told by Tom in sales that an engineer named Mark would call to arrange an installation. Today is 6 April and nobody has called — the online tracker still says 'engineer scheduled' with no date. I work from home and cannot do my job without the line. This is the first time I'm reporting it but I need a proper update now: confirm whether an engineer will attend today or tomorrow and give me a time window. If you can't, escalate to someone who can. I chose your company for reliability; at the moment I'm left without the basics.

I am writing because I'm elderly, not tech-literate, and utterly bewildered by the bill and the termination charge you've put against my account. Back in January I moved into sheltered accommodation and needed to cancel the line — I was told over the phone on 02/02 by Sarah that there would be a small admin fee and that someone would call back. Then on 09/02 Tom told me the charge would be waived because of my situation, and on 17/02 a different advisor said there was nothing showing in the notes and I'd have to pay £220 to leave the contract early. I've rung four times in the last three weeks (02/02, 09/02, 17/02 and 25/02) and every person gives me a different story. I cannot manage apps or websites to sort this myself and I'm getting bills for a service I can no longer use. Please cancel the contract with the reasonable waiver I was promised on 09/02, or explain clearly who will come to help me in person — but do not put me through another runaround.

I am writing because on 14 February 2026 I rang to cancel my landline; the adviser (Sarah) confirmed the cancellation would take effect from 20 February and that my final bill would be pro rata. I am housebound due to limited mobility and cannot easily attend a shop or endure long automated menus, so I relied on that confirmation. Despite this a direct debit of £56.80 was taken on 28 February and a further entry for £32 labelled 'early termination' appears on my account dated 01 March. This is my first contact about the matter; I am asking you to remove the £32 charge and post a closure notice to my registered address so I have a physical record of the account being closed.

I’m calling about how long it takes to get through to your support team — I can’t keep doing 45–60 minute waits. I tried to call on 15/04/2026 at 10:30am about a lost voicemail and was on hold for 48 minutes before I had to hang up because my hands began to cramp (I have repetitive strain injury). The next day, 16/04 at 9:05am, I waited 35 minutes, listened to the same hold music loop, and again had to disconnect. I use the phone service to manage appointments and prescriptions; being on indefinite hold is not just irritating, it’s a barrier to care. I don’t use the app because reading small print on screens causes my dizziness to worsen, so telephone access is essential for me. This is my first formal complaint about wait times, but I need a practical alternative — a guaranteed callback slot, priority routing, or a scheduled time when an advisor will ring me back rather than making me hang on for half an hour. I’ll accept a single dedicated callback within three working days and an assurance you’ll log my account as needing a more accessible contact method. If that’s not possible, advise me how to book a phone appointment so I’m not left in limbo again.

I switched over from my previous provider on 1 March 2026 and since then I've been billed twice for the same month. My March bill shows two line rents of £28.99, totalling an extra £28.99 I did not expect. I have contacted support three times — on 5 March via web chat, on 9 March by phone, and again on 15 March through the app — and each time I was told it would be corrected, but the duplication still appears in my account. It's causing unnecessary stress while I'm trying to settle other moving costs, and I would like a refund for the duplicate charge and confirmation that it will not affect my credit record. Please correct the billing and send a revised statement today.

My name is Ernest Finch. This isn’t just a technical fault — it’s a sequence of small failures that has left me without a reliable phone and confused. I bought a handset through you in December last year (you delivered a model called the ClearCall 7), and for the past month it has been crashing, the touch screen becomes unresponsive and the battery drops from 80% to 5% within hours. I called once before on 18 March and spoke with Alex in technical support; Alex opened ticket F-99823 and said a replacement would be posted out straight away. That replacement never arrived and when my daughter rang to check last week she was told a courier would call — again nothing. I am 78, live alone and am not confident with technology; I need either a working replacement sent by recorded delivery or someone to come and set it up for me at home. I don’t want vague promises — please arrange a home visit or a same-day replacement and call me back on the landline by late afternoon with confirmation. This is my second request and it’s becoming untenable.

Dear Sir or Madam,

I am writing calmly but with urgency about the impact of your 3G switch-off on equipment that I depend on for health reasons. I use a medical alert pendant that connects via 3G and provides a lifeline should I fall; it stopped working after 14/03/2026. I attempted to resolve this by telephone on 15/03/2026 (spoke briefly to Nathan who logged a case), then again on 17/03/2026 (spoke with Priya in technical who advised a replacement SIM but could not confirm postage date). I have also completed the online complaints form and visited my local store; none of these actions has restored dependable service.

I am not comfortable with complex handset changes and need an immediate, practical remedy. Please escalate this matter to a manager or the Ombudsman and provide either an alternative connection that supports my pendant or a loan device until a permanent solution is in place. I would appreciate a named contact and a clear schedule for delivery within three working days, as this is a safety issue for me.

Thank you for your prompt attention.

Kind regards,
Margaret Lane
Account number ending 990

My business depends on having reliable phone and broadband. I cancelled my Business Plus 100 bundle on 02/03/2026 because I was switching premises and arranged porting with a new provider. I spoke with Harriet in Sales on 02/03 and was given cancellation reference CXL-78421; she confirmed that my direct debit would be stopped and final billing calculated pro rata. Despite that, you have taken a full month’s payment of £145 on 01/04/2026 (see transaction reference DD-998244 on my bank statement) and issued invoice INV-202604 for £145.00. This charge is incorrect and is currently tying up funds I need for wage payments and supplier invoices. I have paperwork proving the cancellation and the porting date, and I have not used the service since 15/03/2026. Please reverse the £145 immediately, confirm that no further payments will be taken, and provide written confirmation that my account has been closed with no penalties. If this is not resolved within five working days I will have to escalate through my accountant and consider formal action — I can’t afford ongoing uncertainty when my business relies on cashflow.

I'm a full-time student on a very tight budget and I'm appalled at the unexplained charges on my March bill. On 04/03/2026 I noticed a £24.99 "installation/admin" fee on top of my usual £12 student plan. I have no installation to request — I've had the same SIM for two years. I phoned three different times: first with Hannah on 05/03 (she said she'd investigate and email me a breakdown), then with James, a supervisor, on 10/03 (who promised a refund if the charge couldn't be justified), and finally I sent an email to customerrelations@ on 15/03 after receiving nothing. Each time I've been given conflicting information and no itemised bill. Account number 78-442-129. I have exhausted normal channels and need this escalated to a manager immediately. If you cannot produce a clear explanation and full refund of the £24.99 within seven days I will take the matter to the Ombudsman and submit a formal complaint with supporting call transcripts and my bank statements. Please refund the charge, provide an itemised invoice for March, and confirm what steps you're taking to stop these surprise fees happening to students on fixed incomes.

I'm a student on a fairly tight budget and bought a handset through your upgrade offer on 4 March 2026. Since about 20 March the touchscreen has become intermittently unresponsive and the battery dies after a couple of hours, which makes it difficult to keep up with coursework and part-time shifts. This is the first time I'm raising it with you — I haven't called previously — so I'm just asking whether you can offer a repair, a replacement handset, or a loan phone while this is dealt with. I can't afford an out-of-pocket repair fee right now, so a straightforward solution would be appreciated.

I have been attempting to cancel my service because my health condition makes maintaining this contract difficult, but the process has been repeatedly obstructed by the phone menu and a lack of follow-through. I first phoned on 2 March and spoke to Daniel who said he would email cancellation instructions; I called again on 9 March (Ravi), 16 March (Priya) and most recently on 23 March (line reference 99302), each time being promised a confirmation email that never arrived. Navigating the IVR is challenging for me and I specifically asked for an accessible alternative, which was noted but not implemented. I would like this account terminated and a confirmation sent to my email within five working days. If you cannot process the cancellation, please provide a named contact who will confirm a definite cancellation date and a refund of any overpayment. I am hopeful this can be resolved without further calls.

I switched over from my previous provider on 01/02/2026 specifically because your adverts and the sales agent promised improved indoor reception and reliable data on the 30GB SIM plan. Since porting my number, however, the reality has been a steady degradation: daily dropped calls at home, inability to send or receive MMS, and 3G/4G speeds plunging to unusable levels. Examples: on 04/03/2026 I missed an employer call at 14:40 due to a complete call drop; on 09/03/2026 between 10:00–10:20 my data stalled at 0.1Mbps while trying to upload a timesheet; on 15/03/2026 video calls failed repeatedly. I have logged the issue on multiple occasions — calls on 05/03 (Zara, ticket 621-03), 08/03 (Ahmed, ticket 623-07), a live chat on 12/03 (transcript LC-03-12) and a further call with Tom on 19/03 — each time I was told the case would be escalated and that an engineer or network team would contact me. No engineer has attended and there has been no substantive technical feedback. I have also run your diagnostics app and recorded RSRP readings showing -110 to -118 dBm inside my flat (logs available). For reference, my prior supplier delivered steadier indoor levels and allowed me to work from home reliably; that improvement did not materialise after switching. I need a site survey carried out at my address, a written summary of your findings, and either a tariff adjustment reflecting the degraded service or a contract exit without charge if you cannot deliver adequate coverage. I want a clear timeline and the name of the person responsible for the investigation — I will provide the diagnostic logs upon request.

Lovely to be billed for March even though I cancelled online and completed my switch to BrightLine on 12/03/2026 — thank you for charging me for a month I no longer have. Your final bill (invoice 4421) shows £58.99 taken on 19/03 despite the port completing 12/03 and written confirmation from your own system that the line was deactivated. This is my first time contacting you about it, so here's your chance to refund £58.99 without me having to chase or complain further. Please reverse the charge immediately and confirm by return; if I have to pursue this through the new supplier or the bank, I'll do so, but I'd prefer you fix your billing mess first.

To whom it may concern,

I am the proprietor of Elm & Co. Florists (account BB-43219) and am writing about repeated incorrect charges on our business account for March 2026. Our contract is Business Connect 100, which was agreed on 01/02/2026. The March invoice (INV-20260301-119) shows an unexplained additional charge of £45.00 labelled "Premium Support" and a separate one-off installation fee of £120.00 (INV-20260315-203) which we were told would be waived as part of the introductory offer.

This is the fourth time I have raised this matter: telephone contacts were logged on 02/03 (John in billing), 11/03 (email from accounts), 16/03 (telephone with Maria, who promised a correction), and 19/03 (call reference 78-19-03). Each time I have been given different explanations and no credit. As a small business that depends on your lines for customer orders, these unpredictable charges are directly affecting cashflow. I need a clear audit trail showing why these charges were applied, confirmation that the £165.00 will be refunded immediately to our business account, and assurance that future invoices will reflect the agreed tariff.

I require a formal written response to this email within five working days with the refund reference and corrected invoice. If the error is not resolved within this timeframe I will have to consider escalating to external regulators and seek compensation for any bank fees incurred as a direct consequence. As a small business that depends on your VOIP line for client calls, I am escalating after exhausting the usual support routes. Dear Sir or Madam, I am writing because I have been charged an extra fee that I do not understand and I need this sorted. On 2 March and again on 10 March I raised the matter with your sales team after being enrolled into an add-on service I did not request; the account was modified to include premium device insurance and a contract extension without my explicit consent. I am 78 years old, partially deaf and reliant on my landline for medical calls. I have tried every method you recommend and have received nothing but delays and contradictory information. On 9 February I visited a local shop to use their Wi‑Fi and sent an email which received an automatic acknowledgement only. On 16 February I was told by two different agents that my case was closed; I did not authorise closure. The failure to provide consistent communication and the repeated mislabelling of my case as resolved is unacceptable given my dependence on the service to contact my GP and the ambulance service. Under the Consumer Rights Act 2015 and OFCOM guidance, I am entitled to clear, timely responses and reasonable adjustments because of my age and hearing impairment. I have exhausted your published complaints process. I now require an immediate escalation to a manager with authority to restore reliable service or provide a suitable alternative line within 48 hours. I also require a full written record of every contact your company has logged under my account from 1 January to today, and confirmation of the remedial steps you will take to prevent similar failures with vulnerable customers. If I do not receive a substantive response and a named manager within seven days I will refer this matter to the Ombudsman and pursue every statutory remedy available to me.

I am 78 and not very good with smartphones or apps, so I rely on being able to speak to a person when something goes wrong. On 21 March 2026 I rang your helpline at about 09:30 because the billing section of the app would not load and I needed to check a direct debit. I was on hold for 42 minutes, then the line cut out just as a recorded message said my call would be answered shortly. I sat there following the prompts, convinced there was someone to help, and then nothing. I had to use my neighbour's phone to phone back and by the time I reached an advisor it was nearly lunchtime and I had been shaken. I have never phoned you before. I understand queues happen, but leaving an elderly customer to listen to music and vanish is unacceptable. I want to know why the queue was so long, why calls are allowed to drop without a callback option, and what you will do so I do not have to go through this again. A callback at a time I can manage, preferably before 11am, would be helpful; please don't put me through the automated loop again. I want a clear explanation and a plan to make phone support accessible for people like me.

I am writing because your automated support system has repeatedly failed to diagnose and resolve a billing error that is affecting my business. I first engaged with the chatbot on 5 March to query an extra £360 on the March invoice; the bot closed the session without creating a ticket. On 9 March I tried again; the bot provided a resolution timeline that contradicted the account notes and directed me to pay by 11 March to avoid service interruption. I paid under that instruction, only for an automatic payment block to be applied the next day because the account had been scheduled for manual review — a detail the bot did not reveal.

On 12 March I finally reached a human agent, Daniel, who confirmed there was an underlying tariff misallocation and opened ticket 112233, promising to correct it and waive any late fees. That ticket was auto-closed by the system on 15 March with no action taken, and an engineer appointment that I arranged for 18 March was cancelled because the system showed a cleared balance. The cancellation meant I missed a critical installation at a client site, resulting in lost revenue of £480 and a need to rearrange staff and transport at short notice.

Over three subsequent calls (15 March, 20 March, 22 March) I have had to explain the same sequence each time. The chatbot provided inconsistent instructions, failed to escalate when prompted, and auto-closed legitimate human-raised tickets. I need a full manual audit of all interactions related to invoice March-360, written confirmation of the corrected bill, reimbursement for the missed installation (£480), and a named point of contact who will manage this case to completion. Please treat this as a priority and provide the requested audit and contact details within five working days so I can plan operations accordingly.

Account ending 2371. I have been a customer for over ten years and attempted to cancel my broadband on 4 March 2026; that request was not actioned. I called once before on 20 February 2026 to say I wished to leave. Please process the cancellation and confirm the final chargeable date by return. If you cannot complete this, provide the name and contact details of the person authorised to do so. I expect written confirmation within five working days.

Samantha Brooks, account holder for Family Plus 100 (Account 77‑9983). Your contract states expected speeds up to 100Mbps (typical evening speed 60–80Mbps), yet my household has been consistently receiving 8–12Mbps for the past three weeks. I recorded speed tests on 8 March (9.2Mbps), 12 March (11.1Mbps) and 19 March (8.5Mbps). I reported this on 16 March (ref 55902) and spoke with Ethan, who advised a series of router resets and updated firmware; those actions were completed but have had no effect. With working parents and children doing remote schoolwork, the service level is inadequate for a family plan. This is my second formal request — please arrange a proper line diagnostic and an on‑site engineer visit within the next ten working days, or provide a temporary upgrade at no extra cost while you resolve full throughput. I expect written confirmation of the remedial steps and a firm appointment date.

I signed a 24‑month SIM-only contract on 15 September 2024 at £18.00 per month. On viewing my billing statement on 4 March 2026 I noticed the monthly fee had been increased to £23.00 with no prior notification in the app or by email. As a student on a very tight budget, an unexpected increase of £5 a month makes a difference to whether I can cover essentials this term and I did not agree to any mid-contract rise when I took the plan.

This is the first time I have raised the matter. Please can you supply the exact contractual clause you are relying on to implement this increase, copies of any correspondence you say was sent to me about the change, and a clear calculation showing how the new amount was derived. If the increase cannot be justified under my current terms, I would expect the rate to be reverted to £18.00 and any excess charged since 4 March 2026 to be refunded or credited back to my account. I can wait a short time for a full review but would appreciate acknowledgement of this enquiry within seven working days and a timeline for resolution.

I am writing because my broadband has been consistently slower than the package I pay for and I need assistance that is accessible and straightforward. I subscribe to the 30Mb package but speed checks performed in my home using the router’s diagnostics on 5 March, 12 March and 19 March 2026 repeatedly show download speeds in the range 2–5Mb and frequent drops in connection. I have contacted your support on four separate occasions (telephone 5 March — advisor Neil, 9 March — advisor Paula, 14 March — technical team, and 20 March — supervisor Helen). Each time I have been asked to run tests and restart equipment, which I am able to do, but the underlying problem remains and I am not confident performing more advanced troubleshooting. Given my limited familiarity with technology I request an engineer visit at a mutually convenient time, simple written instructions about any changes made, and a clear explanation if the service at my address cannot meet the advertised speed. I would appreciate a timely appointment within the next week and a confirmation call rather than further automated messages.

I moved into a shared house while studying and thought signing up with your student discount would be straightforward. It has not been. I’m Liam Patel, student, account 99214, and my broadband has been intermittent since 02 February 2026. The story: on 03 February I phoned and an agent called Mark promised an engineer would attend between 8am and 12pm the next Monday; the engineer never arrived. Mark said he would ring back. He did not. On 10 February I phoned again and a different advisor, Claire, booked a new visit for 17 February and said the fault would be fixed then. On 17 February an engineer called me to say the issue was with external cabling and that a third party needed to be scheduled; he left without completing any work and promised a follow-up. No one followed up. I spent two weeks using my phone as a hotspot and spent over £60 of my limited budget topping up data. I have deadlines and an exam on 18 March for which I need stable internet to submit coursework. When I phoned on 05 March to complain I was promised a credit for the period of outage and a guaranteed engineer slot within 48 hours; neither materialised. Each promise has been a promise kept only by your script, not by action. I am paying full student rates for intermittent service and have lost time and money because of your broken commitments. This is not a request; it is a demand for a manager to review the sequence of missed appointments, for an immediate onsite engineer visit before 48 hours, for the agreed credit to be applied to my bill, and for a written apology sent to my university email. If you cannot open a dependable line before my exam I will escalate to the Ombudsman and seek reimbursement for the extra data charges and any resubmission penalties I incur. I do not have the luxury of waiting for another empty promise.

As the proprietor of a small retail business I must record a formal complaint about persistent poor mobile coverage at my premises in Walthamstow, which has been ongoing since December 2025. I have raised the matter on four separate occasions (10/01/2026, 24/01/2026, 07/02/2026 and 28/02/2026) and spoke with Samir and Laura in customer services, who logged faults reference F-987654 and F-991022. The service description in my contract sets out an expectation of reasonable network availability and the failure to provide usable signal is affecting my ability to accept orders and confirm deliveries. Under the contract and the Consumer Rights Act, I require one of the following remedies within 30 days: a demonstrable improvement in coverage at the premises, a proportionate rebate for loss of service, or the right to terminate the contract without penalty. Please provide a technical assessment specific to my address (Unit 3, 42 High Street) and confirm what remedial action you will take and within what timeframe. If you are unable to resolve the problem, advise the procedure for terminating the agreement and any entitlement to compensation under your service guarantee.

I have contacted you four times about the auto-renewal that charged my card on 20/03/2026 despite me cancelling in the app weeks earlier. I am a tech person — I removed the saved card on 02/03, disabled auto-renew in Settings on 05/03 and revoked the app token from my phone on 08/03. Each time I was told differently: Priya said it would be fixed and gave ticket INC-3427 on 10/03; James on 14/03 insisted the payment was authorised by me; Helena on 18/03 promised a refund and that it would not happen again. None of that happened. The charge went through on 20/03 and shows as "subscription renewal" on my bank statement. I have screenshots of the app showing auto-renew off at 05:03 on 05/03 and of the card being removed at 14:12 on 02/03. This is not acceptable. I do not consent to renewals I explicitly disabled. I want an immediate full refund, confirmation that my account is closed to renewals, and written assurance that you have fixed whatever in your billing system allowed a removed card to be charged. If this cannot be resolved within five business days escalate to a manager. I am tired of repeating the same facts to different people and being given contradictory answers — stop wasting my time.

I am raising a formal complaint in relation to persistent poor voice and data coverage at my property (service address: 18 Highfield Road), which began on or about 10 March. I have been a customer of your network for over a decade and this is the first time I have had to put concerns in writing. Under the Consumer Rights Act and the terms of my contract, I am entitled to the service I pay for. Repeated dropouts and inability to make emergency calls are not acceptable. Please provide evidence of the network performance in my postcode, the dates and results of any mast tests, and the proposed remedial works timetable. If you cannot restore acceptable service within seven days I will require a pro-rata refund for the period of reduced service and will consider referral to the Communications Ombudsman for a binding resolution. I expect a written response within 72 hours setting out specific remedies and timescales.

I have exhausted every standard route and now request escalation to a senior manager and the Communications Ombudsman if this cannot be resolved immediately. I am a student on a strict budget and your 3G network shutdown has rendered my mobile internet dongle useless. I opened complaints on 05/02/2026 (agent Nadia, case 5521), 12/02/2026 (Tom, who said he would send a SIM), and 02/03/2026 (email to support). Each time I was given conflicting information and no workable remedy. My contract is for mobile data and you assured coverage at point of sale in September 2025. There was no individual notice to customers with older devices and no reasonable notice to allow me to obtain an affordable replacement. The result: I have missed part-time shifts and could not submit assignments on time because I had no internet. My out-of-pocket cost to replace equipment immediately is more than I can afford. I request immediate options: either (1) provide me with a compatible device or data SIM at no charge within five working days; or (2) a full refund of the unused portion of my contract with no early-termination penalty. If you maintain that your position is lawful, set out the contractual clause you rely on and a copy of the notice you claim was sent to me. I will not accept generic responses. If this is not resolved within seven days I will lodge a formal complaint with Ofcom and the Independent Communications Adjudicator, and pursue any statutory remedies available to me.

This starts with a small, ordinary morning and ends with me in tears at 8.30am because your phone system would not let me fix a direct debit. On Monday 16 March I tried to change the family plan payment to cover the new line for my daughter. I dialled at 7.10am while the kids made porridge. Phone in hand, spoon in other, I waited. After 40 minutes I was told the right department was closed and my call would be queued for a call back. No call. I called again at 8.00pm that evening and was cut off after 25 minutes. I have now exhausted the normal channels — web chat, the app, three calls — and still have no confirmation that our direct debit has been updated. My partner needs confirmation before school trips this week; I cannot keep juggling calls between school runs. I want this escalated to a manager immediately and I want a written confirmation that the direct debit change has been processed; if you cannot resolve this promptly I will take the matter to the ombudsman. I have complaint refs 2026-0316-A and 2026-0316-B if that helps you find the record.

Account number 22-007-811. This is my first formal contact regarding an unauthorised renewal charge but I wish to set the record straight immediately. On 05/03/2026 my SIM Only 20 plan was renewed and £45 was taken from my debit card without my explicit consent to an automatic rollover. Under the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 and relevant guidance on automatic renewals, consent must be clear and unambiguous. I have been a customer since 2008 and no notification advising a change to an auto-renewal mechanism was received. I require a full refund of £45 within 14 days and confirmation that any future renewals will only occur with my express agreement. Please provide a written explanation of the contractual basis you are using to justify this charge and a copy of any purported consent you rely upon. If you fail to respond satisfactorily I will consider escalating this to the Communications Ombudsman and seeking further remedies available under consumer protection law.

I have exhausted your standard support channels and need this escalated to a manager or, if necessary, the ombudsman. On 4 March I reported that my number had been ported away without my authorisation. I opened a case on 11 March, then followed up on 15 March and 20 March via phone and chat, speaking to agents Zoe and Mark; each time I was assured it would be resolved but the number remains inactive and unauthorised charges appeared on 10 March and 12 March. I filed a police report on 18 March (reference PR123456) and provided the details to your team.

At this point routine support has not restored service or provided a satisfactory explanation. I am requesting a named manager to review the account and contact me directly within 48 hours with a plan to restore the number, clear the incorrect charges, and outline steps you will take to prevent a recurrence. If I do not receive that response I will escalate the matter to the ombudsman for independent review.

I have been a customer for just over twelve years and I am setting out the sequence of events because it illustrates why the current billing is both unexpected and unacceptable. In late January 2026 I decided to cancel my home broadband and mobile line after moving to a new address where another provider could give faster service. On 01/02/2026 I called retention and was given a cancellation reference (cancel-010226) and told the return box for equipment would be sent within three working days. The kit was collected by courier and a receipt was emailed to me on 08/02/2026.

Despite that, I was billed on 28/02/2026 for a further month’s broadband fee of £45 and then received a final demand for £90 on 14/03/2026. Over the past six weeks I have chased this matter on four separate occasions: call with Daniel on 03/02, email to billing on 12/02, follow-up with Priya in complaints on 25/02 and a voicemail left on 10/03. Each interaction ended with assurances that the charges would be reversed; none were. I have kept the cancellation reference and the courier receipt and can forward them. After more than a decade of business together I am surprised to be dealing with repeated billing errors and the time spent chasing them.

I am asking for an immediate reversal of the incorrect charges, written confirmation that my account is closed, and an explanation of why the billing system continued to bill following confirmed cancellation. I am willing to discuss this by phone but prefer written confirmation of the outcome. If this is not resolved promptly I will have to consider further steps.

Since switching to your service on 1 February 2026 I have repeatedly been charged fees that were not disclosed during the sale. On my first bill dated 28 February there is an unexpected 'activation fee' of £29.99 and a 'network admin charge' of £12.50; neither was mentioned in the online checkout or by the sales representative. I have contacted customer services on 3 occasions (5 March, 11 March and 19 March 2026) and asked for itemised justification; each time I was promised a full breakdown and then received no adequate response. Under the Consumer Contracts (Information, Cancellation and Additional Charges) Regulations 2013 you are obliged to provide clear information about such charges at the point of contract. I therefore request a full refund of the undisclosed charges, a corrected final bill, and a written explanation of how these charges were allowed to appear. If I do not receive a substantive reply and refund within 14 days I will consider making a formal complaint to the appropriate regulator and seeking redress through the relevant consumer channels.

I am writing to report an instance of fraudulent activity linked to my account and to request formal escalation. On 10 March I received a call purporting to be from your security team and was persuaded to disclose a one‑time passcode; within 24 hours my account was used to transfer £320 to an unknown payee. I have already spoken to your fraud team on 11 March (case ref 76219) and again on 15 March when I was told the matter would be investigated. Both advisors — Hannah in fraud and Raj in customer services — acknowledged the unauthorised transaction but no refund or firm outcome has been provided and I have been advised that ordinary channels are exhausted. As an elderly customer who is not confident with online banking or complex passwords, this has left me without a reliable line and without the disputed funds. Please escalate this matter to a manager immediately and confirm whether you will refer the complaint to the Financial Ombudsman; I expect a written response within seven working days setting out your proposed restitution and the steps you will take to prevent further misuse of my account.

I am 72 and not very good with smartphones, but I can tell when the service is failing. For the past three months, calls drop and 4G disappears in my bungalow on Foxglove Close, BN3 8RT. I have called support on 4 occasions (12 Feb, 3 Mar, 17 Mar, 31 Mar) and spoke with advisors called Raj and Leah who advised network checks and a new SIM. Neither suggestion has helped. My landline is my lifeline; although mobile is not strictly essential, poor coverage means I cannot reliably ring family or the surgery when I need to.

Please arrange a site check or provide a credible plan to improve signal at my address. If a signal booster or alternative solution is available, confirm eligibility and lead time. I appreciate there may be technical constraints, but repeated standard troubleshooting has not fixed this and I would like a clear next step within 10 working days.

I have called your helpline three times in the last month — on 2 March, 9 March and most recently on 16 March — to report the same problem: my phone simply loses signal in my sitting room. Apparently my postcode (RG6 5BX) is not of interest to the network, because every evening my calls drop and texts take hours to arrive. The last agent, Phil, suggested I move to a different room; helpful, but not exactly a long-term fix for someone who prefers to stay seated. I can manage voicemail and my landline, but relying on being able to use the mobile for appointments and shopping is sensible. I was told twice that an engineer visit would be scheduled; no date has been given. I suppose I could take up residence by the back door if that helps your coverage statistics, but realistically I need a practical solution: either improve the indoor signal or offer a temporary alternative while you investigate. I would like confirmation of the next steps and a clear timeframe.

I have been a customer since 2014 and I have a progressive mobility condition that makes budgeting and routine extremely important. On my most recent bill, dated 01 March 2026, I was charged £6 a month more than the rate I was given when I joined. I have checked your website and new customers are being offered a lower price for the same package. I phoned your general enquiries line this morning to ask why long-term customers are being charged more; your automated menu would not transfer me to accounts and the online tariff page is unclear. I need a full explanation of why my price has increased, why there was no individual notice explaining this change, and an immediate review to match the advertised new-customer rate back to March 1st. I rely on a fixed income and occasional home care; small, unexplained increases matter. This feels like a penalty for staying loyal. I am asking you to correct this, refund the difference from 01/03/2026 and confirm in writing what my ongoing monthly charge will be. If you need to contact me, call my landline — I prefer not to use online chat because of my condition. This is my first formal contact about the matter but I will escalate if I do not receive a clear, reasonable response within 14 days.

Hi — I'm Tom Li, and my mobile data has been intermittently dropping for the past two weeks. I raised a ticket through the app on 08/03/2026 (ticket #987654) after seeing download speeds fall to 0.2 Mbps while on 4G in central Manchester — after running multiple speedtests and swapping SIMs between two phones the problem looks network-side. I have performed all standard checks (airplane toggle, APN settings, local signal map, and a factory network reset) and supplied diagnostic logs via your diagnostics tool on 09/03/2026. To date there has been no substantive update in the portal and the ticket remains listed as "under investigation". I work as a consultant and rely on stable mobile data for client calls; this uncertainty is beginning to impact billable hours. Please provide a technical status update, the expected resolution timeline, and whether this will be prioritised. I would like a response within 48 hours with either a fix or a scheduled engineering action.

I have been with you for more than ten years. Today I am writing because of what I can only describe as a cascade of failures by your automated front line. At 14:07 on 23 March I started a chat session via the app, session ID 7c9a21b2 — the transcript shows the chatbot asking me to verify my postcode, then ignoring the verification and suggesting a full equipment reset. I provided a screenshot of the error message (voucher shows 10 days remaining, app display shows 0), and the bot responded with the same troubleshooting checklist three times in succession. At 14:23 I asked to speak to a human. The bot replied that an agent would be with me shortly, then closed the window and emailed me a canned 'we thought we solved your issue' note at 14:30. I called support at 15:05 and the agent, Marcus, confirmed he could see the transcript but could not access the diagnostic logs the bot referenced. He said he would escalate to Tier 2; the reference number he gave is 48291. This is my first formal complaint about the matter. I am not seeking immediate compensation — the fault is minor — but I am extremely frustrated by the lack of coherent handover between automated systems and human staff. The bot will not surface the correct information to agents, agents cannot access the logs the bot cites, and I am left repeating myself. Please investigate the session continuity problem, supply the Tier 2 update that Marcus promised, and outline the steps you will take to ensure that automated triage actually hands cases to humans where appropriate. I expect a technical response detailing session ID 7c9a21b2 and the diagnostic outcome, otherwise I will escalate through your formal complaints procedure.

Dear Customer Services,

I am contacting you as the account holder for Family Plan ID 77-438-900. I manage this mobile plan for my family (4 lines) and have been a customer for several years. On 03/03/2026 I queried my monthly bill with your adviser Hannah R. (call reference CRN-232903) who advised she would escalate because the plan price applied to my account, £38.99 per month, appears to be significantly higher than a promotional rate currently marketed to new customers (£26.99 per month) for the same ‘Together 50’ product.

This is my second attempt to obtain a satisfactory explanation. I raised the matter informally by telephone and was told it would be reviewed; no adjustment or further correspondence has been received. I am requesting a formal review of my account charges, a retrospective credit for any difference since 01/01/2026, and an assurance that my plan will be aligned with equivalent new-customer tariffs or an alternative loyalty offer. Please respond with your decision and calculations within 14 days. If you require further documentation, advise promptly so I can provide it. I expect a clear, professional resolution without further delay.

Yours sincerely,
Daniela Browne

I have exhausted your standard complaint channels and require escalation.

Account name: Sarah Malik. Mobile number: 07412 958034. I am a student on a limited income. On 18 February 2026 I phoned your support line and spoke with James Carter (call ref 18FEB-2203). He confirmed that my tariff would not increase until the end of my 12‑month fixed period but that the system showed a price rise effective 1 March. I raised this again with Customer Resolutions on 26 February (CR-9981) and received no substantive reply.

This appears to be a breach of the fixed-term pricing terms set out in clause 4.2 of my contract dated 02/03/2025. Your unilateral application of a higher rate before the term ends is not permitted without my explicit consent. I am therefore formally requesting: (a) immediate correction of my billing to reflect the contracted price from 01/03/2026; (b) refund of any overpayments; and (c) a written confirmation that my tariff will remain at the contracted rate for the remainder of the fixed term.

If you do not provide a satisfactory resolution within 14 calendar days of this message I will escalate to the independent adjudicator and pursue the matter through the Communications Ombudsman. I am attaching copies of the contract page, the two call references and the March invoice that shows the higher charge. Please transfer this matter to a senior manager for urgent review and confirm receipt and intended timetable for action by return.

I have been a customer since 2019, and the progression to this point feels like a book I didn't sign up to read. Freshers' term, 2019: I signed up because the student deal was reasonable and your staff promised loyalty would be recognised. Fast forward to 2026. Tuition fees have gone up; rent eats most of my grant; yet my monthly bill has grown while the adverts outside campus show new customers getting better deals. I first raised this with customer services on 02/03 (case 361-02-03) and spoke to Tom on 06/03 who said someone from retention would call. They did not. I have called back three times since — names I remember are Rhianna and Marcus — and each time I am put through hoops: re-verification, 'currently reviewing your account', suggestions to switch to a bundle I cannot afford, and automated offers that require me to transfer existing contract terms to a new plan. I have proof of better deals for new customers: the price gap is almost a fiver a month, which matters when I'm budgeting for food and books. I am not asking for charity. I am asking for fair treatment: either apply the best available student rate to my plan from next billing cycle, or give me a clear, penalty-free way to leave. Tell me which it will be and do it by 30 March, otherwise I will take this further with Citizens Advice and my university's student union.

I switched from BrightWave on 2026-01-15 and took out your Home Broadband Plus package (account 87-3419-PL). My first bill, dated 2026-02-25 (invoice 20260234), includes a "new customer setup fee" of £39.99 and a line rental surcharge of £6.50 that were never disclosed when I signed up over the phone on 2026-01-12 with advisor Hannah. When I queried this on 2026-03-02 I was told by 'Tom' in billing that the setup fee was a one-off admin charge and would be refunded; that refund has not appeared. I have now called the complaints line three times (03/03 with Mia, 10/03 with Raj, 18/03 with Sasha), raised web chat case IDs CW-3321 and CW-3478, and been given inconsistent explanations about tariffs and microcharges. I have exhausted the usual channels and need a clear audit of every line item applied since activation, a full refund of the £46.49 extra charged, and a written assurance that no further undisclosed fees will be billed. Please escalate this matter to a manager and, if you cannot resolve it promptly, I will pursue referral to the Ombudsman. I expect a response within five working days with confirmation and a timetable for refunding the amount.

I have called about the mid-contract price increase four times over the last three weeks: on 02 March 2026 (case no. 834210), 09 March 2026 (advisor Saira Khan), 16 March 2026 (advisor Tom Herbert) and again on 22 March 2026. Each advisor confirmed they had logged my complaint but so far the increase that took effect on 10 March 2026—from £28.99 to £33.99 per month—has not been reversed. My contract, signed electronically on 01 October 2025, contains no clause permitting unilateral increases outside agreed review windows unless I am given explicit notice and the option to terminate without penalty. I require an immediate reinstatement of the original tariff or a formal notice explaining the legal basis for this adjustment, together with confirmation that you will waive any exit fees should I choose to leave as a result. I am able to escalate this to the relevant regulator if necessary, but I would prefer a prompt commercial resolution within five business days.

I raised this once on 9 April with Ben on the retention team and was told nothing could be done. That answer sat badly with me so I’m writing again. I’m on the same package I signed up to three years ago yet new customers are being offered better deals for identical speeds and allowances. I have proof of the current offers on your website and screenshots of my last bill. I don’t care for excuses about ‘system rules’. Match the advertised price for new customers, apply the difference as a credit from 1 May, and explain why loyalty is being charged a premium. If you can’t, tell me plainly so I can move providers.

I am writing again because your withdrawal of 3G services on 12 March 2026 has left my phone unreliable and you have not provided a lawful or contractual remedy. I have reported this problem three times so far: first to Helen in Customer Care on 14 March (case 342198), then to Raj via webchat on 18 March, and most recently to the retention team on 22 March when I insisted on escalation. Each time I was promised a technical review and a specific timescale; none of those timescales were met and I received no substantive update.

My contract makes no provision for being moved to inferior service without compensation or an option to exit. My handset relies on 3G fallback for secure hardware token connectivity, which failed repeatedly between 12–15 March, causing me to miss two client calls and to be locked out of work applications. That is a predictable consequence of withdrawing a network layer without adequate migration support. I am a paying customer who planned around the service you sold me. Your unilateral change to service capability amounts to a variation of contract.

I am requesting the following within seven days: a formal written explanation of the technical reason for service loss; a clear statement of my contractual options (including immediate exit without penalty); and compensation equivalent to the proportion of my monthly bill for the period in which the service has been degraded. If you cannot supply these, please escalate this to your legal or regulatory complaints team and provide a named contact. If I do not receive a satisfactory response by 1 April 2026 I will refer this matter to Ofcom and pursue remedies under the Consumer Rights Act 2015. I do not want further automated responses; provide a named individual and a timetable.

I moved over from my previous provider because I wanted a straightforward unlimited data plan, and since 05/03/2026 I have been chasing the fact your system lists me on a different tariff. I have called three times — 07/03, 14/03 and this morning 22/03 — and spoke with Mark and then Leah, who both promised corrections would be made. The problem remains: my account shows a 200GB cap and a premium channel bundle I did not order, and I have received a bill for an extra £28.50 this month. I’m not looking for a lecture; I just want the plan I signed up for applied to my account, the incorrect charges refunded, and email confirmation that the cap and unwanted extras have been removed. Please action this and confirm by return so I can stop checking my account every day.

I am writing after exhausting your frontline teams and automated routes. Over the last three billing cycles I have been charged a series of previously undisclosed items: a so-called 'service administration' fee of £7.50 on 28 January 2026 and 28 February 2026, a 'router activation' charge of £45 on 14 January despite my plan including a free router, and an unexplained 'account maintenance' entry of £3.99 on 1 March.
